name: Baldur's Ship
description: Baldur's ship, most famously known as Hringhorni, was the largest ship ever built, a vessel of immense size and grandeur. It was the ship used for Baldur's funeral pyre. Given Baldur's status as the fairest and most beloved of the Aesir gods, Hringhorni's selection reflected the profound sorrow and respect for the fallen deity. So large was the ship that even the strongest of the gods, including Thor, could not budge it from its place. Ultimately, the giantess Hyrrokkin was summoned from Jotunheim. With immense supernatural power, she pushed the ship into the water with such force that the earth trembled and sparks flew from the rollers. The ship then became Baldur's funeral pyre, where he was laid with his wife Nanna, who died of grief, and many treasures. The significance of Hringhorni lies not in any inherent magical properties, but in its function as the vessel of passage for Baldur's spirit to the afterlife. It symbolizes the end of an era of light and beauty in Asgard, and the foreshadowing of Ragnarok. The ship's immense size also reflects Baldur's importance and the impact of his death on the cosmos. It represents the scale of grief felt by the gods. Its association with Hyrrokkin highlights the ambivalent relationship between the Aesir and the giants, who are sometimes allies and sometimes adversaries. The burning of Hringhorni marked a pivotal moment in Norse mythology, signifying loss, respect, and the inevitable cycle of death and rebirth.
subtitle: Vessel of Grief and Final Passage
origin: Built for Baldur, primarily used for his funeral pyre.
currentLocation: Burned at sea, its remains scattered or lost to the waves.

keyMyths.title: Baldur's Funeral
keyMyths.description: Hringhorni was used as Baldur's funeral pyre, symbolizing the end of his life and the beginning of the events leading to Ragnarok. Nanna, his wife, died of grief and was also placed on the ship.

keyMyths.title: Hyrrokkin's Strength
keyMyths.description: The gods were unable to launch the massive ship until the giantess Hyrrokkin arrived and effortlessly pushed it into the water, showcasing her immense strength. This highlights the power of the giants.

sources.author: Snorri Sturluson
sources.description: A key source for Norse mythology, including the description of Baldur's funeral and Hringhorni.
sources.text: A key source for Norse mythology, including the description of Baldur's funeral and Hringhorni.

sources.author: Unknown
sources.description: Collection of Old Norse poems, some of which allude to Baldur's death and funeral customs.
